segments.voice: On the night of November 7, 1991, in Vienna, Austria, a 34-year-old bartender named Astrid Kollmann left her shift at a small bar in the city's 16th district. She was last seen waiting at a tram stop near her workplace. She never reached home. Her case has remained unsolved for over three decades.

Kollmann lived in the Ottakring neighborhood, a working-class area in northwest Vienna. She had moved to the city from Lower Austria several years earlier. She had been working at the bar for about a year. Coworkers described her as friendly, careful, and reliable.

On the night she disappeared, Kollmann had finished her shift around 1 a.m. The bar where she worked was on Thaliastrasse, a busy thoroughfare with multiple tram lines. She regularly took the same route home — a tram followed by a short walk.
Witnesses at the tram stop reported seeing her waiting. Then she was no longer there. She had not boarded the tram she normally took. Her apartment was less than three kilometers from the bar.

By midday on November 8, 1991, Kollmann's family realized she had not arrived home. She had a young daughter who was being cared for by her mother that night. The family contacted the Bundespolizei — the Austrian federal police.
Vienna police canvassed the area immediately. They interviewed bar patrons. They checked hospitals, train stations, and morgues. They circulated her photograph through the city.
Her purse, identification, and personal effects were never found. No body was recovered. No witnesses came forward describing any altercation at the tram stop.

Here's where the case became frustrating for investigators. Vienna in 1991 had limited CCTV coverage compared to modern cities. The tram stop where Kollmann was last seen was not under camera surveillance. Witness statements were the primary evidence.

Vienna police assigned a homicide team to the case after several weeks. They categorized her disappearance as likely foul play. They began investigating bar patrons, possible romantic acquaintances, and known offenders active in Ottakring at the time.
Several persons of interest were investigated through 1992 and 1993. None were charged. The leads developed through interviews did not produce physical evidence.

By 1994, the active investigation had wound down. Kollmann's family continued to search privately. Her mother distributed flyers throughout Vienna. Local newspapers periodically covered the case on its anniversaries.

The case took on new significance in 2004 when an Austrian convicted serial killer named Wolfgang Ott confessed to multiple killings of women across Vienna and Lower Austria during the 1980s and 1990s. Ott was already serving life in prison for unrelated murders.

Vienna police investigated whether Ott might be responsible for Kollmann's disappearance. Ott initially seemed willing to discuss the case. Then he denied involvement. Then he gave inconsistent statements. Without physical evidence, the link could not be proved.

Some Austrian researchers continue to argue Ott was likely responsible. Others argue the geography and timing of his known crimes do not align well with the Kollmann disappearance. The Bundeskriminalamt, Austria's federal criminal police, has neither confirmed nor ruled out the connection.

Kollmann's daughter, who was a small child when her mother vanished, has spoken publicly about the case in subsequent decades. She has appeared on Austrian television missing-persons programs. Her hope is to recover her mother's remains for proper burial.

Vienna's 16th district has changed considerably since 1991. The bar where Kollmann worked is now a different establishment. The tram stop has been redesigned. Some buildings along Thaliastrasse have been demolished and rebuilt.

The case file is held at the Vienna Bundespolizei homicide division. It remains officially open. Austrian investigators have stated publicly they will pursue any new leads, including DNA evidence from items associated with the case if any can be recovered.

Modern forensic genealogy, of the kind that helped identify the Sumter County Does in Florida, requires biological evidence. Without recovered remains, the technique cannot be applied to Kollmann's disappearance.
Her family has held memorial events on the anniversary of her disappearance most years. The November 7 date has become a touchstone in Austrian missing-persons advocacy.

Kollmann's case is one of several Vienna disappearances from the 1980s and 1990s that remain unresolved. Some Austrian researchers have argued for a serial-offender theory linking multiple cases, though no DNA or witness evidence has connected them.

If you were in Vienna's 16th district on the night of November 7, 1991, particularly near the Thaliastrasse area, the Vienna Bundespolizei continues to accept witness information through its general tip line.

The case is also studied in Austrian criminology as an example of pre-CCTV-era investigative limitations. Modern Vienna has substantially expanded camera coverage in part as a response to cases like Kollmann's.
Astrid Kollmann's photograph remains on the Bundeskriminalamt missing persons portal. She is recorded as missing since November 7, 1991. Her family continues to hope for resolution.
If you have any information that may help, the Bundeskriminalamt accepts tips in German, English, and other major European languages. The case file ID is on the Austrian missing persons portal.
